section.title: Rejection and the Death of John
section.sectionRationale: After the parables, the shadow of rejection deepens. Jesus is dishonored in His own hometown, where familiarity breeds unbelief, and Matthew then recounts the brutal execution of John the Baptist by a weak, guilt-ridden Herod. The forerunner's fate at the hands of a cowardly king foreshadows the path of rejection and death that awaits Jesus Himself.
section.thesis: The One rejected in His hometown by unbelief and the forerunner beheaded by a cowardly king together reveal the world's hostility to God's messengers, so that the death of John casts the shadow of the cross over the ministry of Jesus, the prophet without honor.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Jesus dishonored and hindered by unbelief in Nazareth; Herod's guilty fear that Jesus is John raised; and the account of John's imprisonment and beheading.

verses.text: Coming to His {{patris|hometown}}, He taught the people in their synagogue, and they were astonished. “Where did this man get such wisdom and miraculous powers?” they asked.
“Isn’t this the carpenter’s son? Isn’t His mother’s name Mary, and aren’t His brothers James, Joseph, Simon, and Judas?
Aren’t all His sisters with us as well? Where then did this man get all these things?”
And they {{skandalizo|took offense}} at Him. But Jesus said to them, “Only in his hometown and in his own household is a {{prophetes|prophet}} without honor.”
And He did not do many miracles there, because of their {{apistia|unbelief}}.

units.label: Herod's guilty fear
units.note: this is John, risen from the dead

verses.text: At that time Herod the tetrarch heard the reports about Jesus
and said to his servants, “This is John the Baptist; he has risen from the dead! That is why miraculous powers are at work in him.”
Now Herod had arrested John and bound him and put him in prison on account of Herodias, his brother Philip’s wife,
because John had been telling him, “It is not lawful for you to have her.”
Although Herod wanted to kill John, he was afraid of the people, because they regarded John as a prophet.
On Herod’s birthday, however, the daughter of Herodias danced before them and pleased Herod
so much that he promised with an oath to give to her whatever she asked.
Prompted by her mother, she said, “Give me here on a platter the head of John the Baptist.”
The king was grieved, but because of his oaths and his guests, he ordered that her wish be granted
and sent to have John beheaded in the prison.
John’s head was brought in on a platter and presented to the girl, who carried it to her mother.
Then John’s disciples came and took his body and buried it. And they went and informed Jesus.

patris.gloss: Hometown, native place. Jesus returns to Nazareth, where He grew up, and finds not faith but offense; those who knew Him as the carpenter's son cannot see past the familiar to the glory before them.

skandalizo.gloss: To stumble, take offense, be tripped up. The townspeople are scandalized by Jesus, tripping over His ordinary origins; the same word describes those who fall away, for offense at Christ is a barrier to faith.

prophetes.gloss: Prophet, one who speaks for God. Jesus applies the proverb that a prophet is without honor in his hometown to Himself, placing His rejection in the long line of God's spokesmen dishonored by their own people.

apistia.gloss: Unbelief, lack of faith. It is because of their unbelief that Jesus did not do many miracles in Nazareth; not that His power failed, but that His mighty works met faith, not spectacle for hardened hearts.

basic.context: Jesus returns to Nazareth and teaches with astonishing wisdom, but the people cannot get past knowing His family: is this not the carpenter's son? They take offense, and He does few miracles there because of their unbelief. Matthew then flashes back to how John the Baptist died: imprisoned for rebuking Herod's unlawful marriage, John is beheaded when Herod, trapped by a rash oath at a birthday feast, grants the request of Herodias's dancing daughter.
basic.hermeneutics: Read these two rejections together. The hometown unbelief shows how familiarity can blind people to Christ. The death of John is told as a grim foreshadowing: a faithful prophet killed by a weak ruler to save face echoes what will happen to Jesus. Matthew places John's martyrdom here, out of strict sequence, to deepen the theme of rejection.
basic.originalLanguages: In His hometown Jesus meets not faith but offense; the people stumble over His ordinary origins. He calls Himself a prophet without honor among his own. And He does few miracles there because of their unbelief, not a failure of power but the absence of the faith to which His works answered.
basic.typology: Jesus stands in the line of rejected prophets, dishonored by His own as they were. John dies as Elijah-like prophets often suffered, opposed by a wicked queen, recalling Elijah and Jezebel. And John's martyrdom foreshadows the greater rejection and death of the One he prepared the way for.
basic.christ: Jesus is the prophet without honor, rejected by those who knew Him best, whose true glory is hidden from unbelief. And He is the One whose forerunner's death prefigures His own: John, faithful to the end, is killed by a cowardly king, just as Jesus will be handed over by weak rulers to keep the crowd content.
basic.god: God's messengers are often dishonored and killed, yet God's purposes advance through their faithfulness and even their deaths. He is not thwarted by unbelief or by wicked kings; the rejection of His prophets and His Son is folded into His saving plan.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 19 shows Elijah persecuted by Jezebel, echoed in John and Herodias. Matthew 17 confirms John as the Elijah who suffered. Jeremiah 11 tells of a prophet rejected in his hometown. And Matthew 27 shows Pilate, like Herod, condemning the innocent to please others.
basic.oneStory: The shadow over Jesus' ministry lengthens. Rejected by His own town and reminded of His forerunner's murder, He walks a path the world has always forced on God's messengers. John dies faithful at the hands of a weak king, and his death points ahead to the cross, where the greater Prophet will be handed over by cowardly rulers, yet through it accomplish salvation.

context.literary: The hometown rejection closes the parables section and sets a tone of unbelief. Matthew then narrates John's death as a flashback, prompted by Herod's guilty speculation that Jesus is John raised. The placement deepens the theme of rejection and prepares for Jesus' withdrawal in 14:13. John's fate is told as a foil and forerunner to Jesus' own coming passion.
context.historical: Herod Antipas, tetrarch of Galilee, had married Herodias, his brother's former wife, which John denounced as unlawful under Leviticus 18 and 20. Herod feared John yet was intrigued by him. A ruler's rash oath before guests was a matter of public honor; to break it would mean losing face, which the weak Herod would not do even to spare an innocent life.
context.canonical: Jesus' rejection fulfills the pattern of the prophets dishonored by their own, and He confirms in Matthew 17:12-13 that John was the promised Elijah who suffered. The Elijah and Jezebel conflict, 1 Kings 19:1-2, stands behind John and Herodias. John's death, like the later trial of Jesus before Pilate, shows weak authorities sacrificing the righteous to preserve their standing.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To deepen the theme of rejection by showing Jesus dishonored in His hometown and His forerunner martyred by a cowardly king, so that the reader sees the world's hostility to God's messengers and the shadow of the cross falling across Jesus' ministry.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes historical rejection and martyrdom. It does not prescribe a program but warns against the unbelief that stumbles over the familiar Christ and the moral cowardice of Herod, while holding up John's faithful stand for the truth even unto death as worthy of imitation.

debates.question: Why could Jesus not do many miracles in Nazareth because of their unbelief?

views.label: Not a limit on His power but on His purpose
views.case: Jesus' miracles answered faith and served His mission; He would not perform them as spectacle for hardened hearts, so their unbelief did not weaken His power but removed the fitting occasion for it.

views.label: A real moral connection between faith and blessing
views.case: Scripture repeatedly ties the receiving of God's works to faith; the unbelief of Nazareth genuinely closed them off from the blessings faith would have received, though Jesus' ability was never in question.

debates.bearing: Both affirm Jesus' unlimited power and warn that unbelief shuts people off from the blessings He gives, so that familiarity and offense can rob us of Christ's work among us.

debates.question: What does Herod's execution of John reveal?

views.label: The cowardice of pleasing people over doing right
views.case: Herod knew John was righteous and feared him, yet killed him to keep a rash oath and save face before his guests, a portrait of moral weakness that sacrifices truth to human approval.

views.label: A foreshadowing of Jesus' own fate
views.case: A faithful witness condemned by a reluctant ruler to satisfy others prefigures Pilate handing Jesus over to please the crowd; John's death casts the shadow of the cross ahead.

debates.bearing: Both show the deadly cost of valuing human approval over righteousness and set John's faithful martyrdom as a signpost pointing to the greater rejection of Christ.

study.originalLanguages: In His patris, His hometown, Jesus is met not with faith but with offense; the people skandalizo, stumble, over His familiar origins, unable to see the carpenter's son as more than they remember. He names Himself a prophetes without honor among his own, joining the line of God's dishonored spokesmen. And He does few miracles there because of their apistia, their unbelief, not because His power fails but because His works met faith, not spectacle; where hearts are closed, the blessings faith would receive are forfeited.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus joins the rejected prophets of Israel, dishonored by their own, the pattern that culminates in His crucifixion, John 1:11.
The forerunner opposed by a wicked queen recalls Elijah and Jezebel, and Jesus names John the Elijah who suffered, Matthew 17:12-13.

typology.type: The righteous killed by a weak ruler
typology.fulfillment: John condemned by Herod to save face foreshadows Jesus handed over by Pilate to please the crowd, Matthew 27:24-26.

study.christ: Here Christ is the rejected One, dishonored precisely by those who should have known Him best. His neighbors are astonished at His wisdom and works, yet they trip over His ordinary background and refuse to believe, so that the incarnation itself, God hidden in a familiar Galilean life, becomes a stumbling block. And in the death of John, Matthew sets a signpost toward the cross: the forerunner who prepared the Lord's way also precedes Him in martyrdom, faithful to the truth and killed by a cowardly king. As John was, so the greater Prophet will be, rejected, bound, and handed over by weak rulers, yet accomplishing through His death the salvation John announced.
study.god: The God of this passage is not defeated by unbelief or by the murder of His prophets. He sends His messengers, and when they are dishonored and even killed, His purposes are not overthrown but advanced. The unbelief of Nazareth cannot diminish who Jesus is, and the martyrdom of John cannot silence the truth he spoke. Behind the tragedy stands a sovereign God who folds even the rejection of His servants and His Son into His saving plan, and who will vindicate the faithful who suffer for His name.

crossReferences.note: Elijah hunted by Jezebel, the pattern behind John's persecution by Herodias.
Jesus confirms that John was the promised Elijah, who suffered at the hands of his enemies.
He came to His own, and His own did not receive Him, fulfilled in the hometown rejection.
Pilate, like Herod, condemns the innocent to satisfy others, echoing John's death.

study.oneStory: The road to the cross grows darker. Rejected by His own town and reminded of His forerunner's murder, Jesus walks the path the world has always forced on God's messengers. Nazareth stumbles over the familiar face of God among them, and John dies faithful under a cowardly king. His beheading is a grim rehearsal of what awaits the greater Prophet, who will likewise be handed over by weak rulers to please the crowd, yet who will turn the world's rejection into the very means of its salvation.

quiz.q: Why did the people of Nazareth take offense at Jesus?

options.text: They could not see past His familiar family and ordinary origins to believe in Him.
options.note: Verses 55-57 show them stumbling over the carpenter's son they thought they knew.

options.text: They rejected His teaching as unwise and dull.
options.note: They were astonished at His wisdom; their offense was over His familiar background, not poor teaching.

options.text: He refused to teach in their synagogue.
options.note: He did teach in their synagogue; the offense was their unbelief, not His refusal.

options.text: He performed no wonders anywhere at all.
options.note: He did mighty works elsewhere; in Nazareth He did few because of their unbelief.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus do few miracles in His hometown?

options.text: Because of their unbelief, not because His power had failed.
options.note: Verse 58 names their unbelief as the reason.

options.text: Because He was too tired from teaching.
options.note: The text names unbelief, not weariness, as the reason.

options.text: Because God forbade Him to help His own town.
options.note: No such prohibition; their unbelief closed them off from His works.

options.text: Because He had lost His ability to heal.
options.note: His power was undiminished; the barrier was their unbelief, not any loss of power.

quiz.q: Why had Herod imprisoned John the Baptist?

options.text: Because John rebuked him for unlawfully marrying Herodias, his brother's wife.
options.note: Verses 3-4 give John's rebuke of the unlawful marriage as the cause.

options.text: Because John had led a revolt against Herod's rule.
options.note: John led no revolt; he rebuked Herod's marriage, a moral, not political, offense.

options.text: Because John claimed to be the Messiah.
options.note: John pointed away from himself to Christ; his imprisonment was over rebuking Herod's marriage.

options.text: Because John refused to pay taxes.
options.note: The issue was John's rebuke of the unlawful marriage, not taxes.

quiz.q: How did John come to be executed?

options.text: Herod, trapped by a rash oath at his birthday feast, granted Herodias's daughter's request for John's head.
options.note: Verses 6-10 show the oath, the dance, and the fatal request.

options.text: Herod eagerly ordered John killed at the first opportunity.
options.note: Herod was actually grieved and had feared the people; he killed John reluctantly to keep his oath, not eagerly.

options.text: The people demanded John's execution.
options.note: The people regarded John as a prophet; the demand came from Herodias through her daughter.

options.text: John died of illness in prison.
options.note: John was beheaded, not taken by illness.

quiz.q: What larger significance does the death of John carry in Matthew?

options.text: The faithful forerunner killed by a cowardly king foreshadows Jesus' own rejection and death.
options.note: John's martyrdom prefigures the cross, where weak rulers condemn the innocent Christ.

options.text: It shows that John's ministry had been a failure.
options.note: John was faithful to the end; his death was martyrdom, not failure, and points ahead to Christ.

options.text: It proves Herod was a righteous but misunderstood ruler.
options.note: Herod is portrayed as weak and cowardly, sacrificing the innocent to save face.

options.text: It means God had abandoned His prophets.
options.note: God's purposes advance through the faithfulness and even death of His messengers; He had not abandoned them.

questions: The people of Nazareth were too familiar with Jesus to believe in Him. Where might overfamiliarity be dulling your own wonder at who Jesus is?
Their unbelief closed them off from Jesus' works. How might your own doubt or hardness be limiting what you receive from Him?
John spoke the truth to a powerful man even though it cost him his freedom and his life. Where is God calling you to stand for what is right despite the cost?
Herod valued his reputation and his guests' opinion over an innocent life. In what areas are you tempted to do wrong rather than lose face before others?
John died faithful, and his death pointed ahead to Christ. How does the example of faithful suffering strengthen you to follow Jesus even when it is hard?

translationNotes.note: The carpenter's son and the naming of Jesus' brothers and sisters show His genuinely ordinary upbringing, which became the occasion of Nazareth's unbelief.
John condemned Herod's marriage to Herodias, his brother Philip's wife, as unlawful under Leviticus 18:16 and 20:21.
Herod's grief yet compliance shows a ruler valuing his oath and his guests' opinion above justice, prefiguring Pilate's later condemnation of Jesus to please the crowd.

Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
